sources.notes: Read the complete current statement, examples, walk semantics, and all constraints; H1 permits at most 2n+4 walks.

Read the full official H1 tutorial and complete reference implementation. It derives the key path, anchors a minimum, simulates odd-even transposition phases on the remaining values, restores the minimum with the top-row/right-column path, and handles the rotating frame by parity.

Actually read the complete independent H2 article. Its operation model and H1 discussion independently cross-check that RD/RRDD tiles move the first value to the end while selecting disjoint adjacent swaps, and that H1 spends a second walk per odd-even phase to restore the first value.

Actually read the complete H1 article. It describes shift/shuffle macros and a suffix-building alternative, but explicitly relies on trying ad hoc pre-transformations and supplies no worst-case 2n+4 proof, so it was not used as correctness evidence.

Read the linked community post behind the Luogu H1 alternative. The author explicitly asks how to hack the heuristic and reports only stress testing; it is retained as a cautionary source, not as a validated solution.

Independently proved the key-path permutation, restoring-path permutation, rotating-coordinate reduction to alternating odd-even phases, and walk bound. A path-level simulator checked all permutations through n=8, all arrays over values 1..3 through n=8, and 150 random arrays up to n=500 as part of a 56,214-case joint check. Every route was monotone with exactly 2n-2 moves and every H1 result sorted within 2n-1 walks.

annotation.summary: Given an integer array, output at most 2n+4 monotone paths from (1,1) to (n,n); entering (i,j) swaps a_i and a_j when i differs from j, and the accumulated walks must sort the array nondecreasingly.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= t <= 10000, 2 <= n <= 500, 1 <= a_i <= n, and the sum of n^2 over tests is at most 2.5e5. Every output path has exactly 2n-2 moves, and H1 allows at most 2n+4 paths.
annotation.core_idea: Tile a walk along the diagonal by RD or RRDD. Starting with a carried value a_1, either tile advances it past one value unchanged or past two values while swapping those two; hence one key walk maps [a_1,a_2,...,a_n] to [b_2,...,b_n,a_1], where b is obtained from [a_2,...,a_n] by arbitrary disjoint adjacent swaps. First use one selector-shaped walk to place a minimum at position 1. A key walk can then realize one compare-exchange matching of odd-even transposition sort on the other m=n-1 values, but sends the minimum to position n. The path R^(n-1)D^(n-1) maps [c_1,...,c_m,mn] to [mn,c_m,c_1,...,c_{m-1}], restoring the minimum and rotating the tail right by one. Define the logical tail after r rounds by rotating the physical tail left by r; the parity-adjusted physical matching becomes exactly the alternating odd/even matching in that logical frame, with the wraparound pair omitted. Odd-even transposition sort finishes after m phases, so one optional selector plus two walks for each phase sorts the array in at most 2n-1 walks.
annotation.complexity: O(n^2) time and O(n^2) output size per test; O(n^2) memory when paths are buffered, or O(n) auxiliary memory if streamed.
tricks: View RD and RRDD as diagonal tiles that carry the first value while optionally compare-swapping one disjoint adjacent pair.
Keep a minimum as a sentinel at position 1 between sorting phases.
Analyze the tail in a coordinate frame counter-rotating against the restoring path.
Skip exactly the physical pair that would wrap across the logical tail boundary.
Use strict greater-than compare-exchanges so duplicate values remain valid.
uncertainty_points: No unresolved correctness uncertainty remains for the official odd-even construction after source cross-checks, the independent rotating-frame proof, and path-level exhaustive/random verification.
The independent H1 Luogu/community suffix-building construction has no published worst-case proof for its trial pre-transformations and is intentionally not recorded as a solution variant.
algorithm/sorting/odd-even-transposition-sort is absent from the current registry and is proposed as a reusable candidate; applying this payload requires taxonomy approval and a WebUI translation.
proof_risks: A key walk does not allow arbitrary swaps: it moves the old first value to the end and permits only a matching of disjoint adjacent swaps among the remaining values.
The restoring path also right-rotates the tail. Reusing fixed physical parity pairs without compensating for that rotation does not simulate odd-even transposition sort.
The omitted comparison is the pair crossing the logical boundary after rotation, not an arbitrary inconvenient pair.
The proof needs m=n-1 alternating phases on the tail and one optional minimum-placement walk; the resulting bound is 2n-1, safely below 2n+4.
Duplicate values require compare-exchange on > rather than >=; no permutation assumption is needed for H1.

solution_variants.name: minimum-anchor-rotating-odd-even-sort
solution_variants.summary: Anchor a minimum at the front, encode each odd-even compare-exchange phase into one diagonal-tiled key walk, then use the top-row/right-column walk to restore the minimum while tracking the induced cyclic shift in a logical coordinate frame.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n^2) time and output, at most 2n-1 walks, and O(n^2) buffered or O(n) streaming auxiliary memory.

tags.evidence: The tail is sorted by exactly n-1 alternating odd/even parallel compare-exchange phases; the rotating-frame parity mapping is the central correctness argument.
tags.description: Sort a sequence by alternating parallel compare-exchange phases on pairs (1,2),(3,4),... and (2,3),(4,5),.... Use when this phase schedule or sorting-network structure is essential, not for arbitrary adjacent-swap sorting.
tags.created_reason: Both CF2039H versions encode one odd-even comparison phase into each constrained grid walk; the broad algorithm/sorting tag does not expose this decisive reusable phase-based sorting method.

tags.evidence: The task is solved by explicitly constructing bounded monotone grid paths whose induced swap permutations realize every sorting phase and the minimum-restoration step.
Between phase pairs the minimum is restored to position 1, while the logical tail is defined by a counter-rotation that preserves the interpretation as one fixed odd-even sorting network.
The physical matching and unmatched endpoint depend on n and phase parity so that, after undoing the accumulated rotation, logical odd and even comparison layers alternate correctly.
Each abstract pass, compare-exchange, singleton, and restoring permutation must be serialized into one legal R/D path of exactly 2n-2 moves without crossing the grid boundary.
